coredata.dc:title: CMC/Starch/SiO2/ GQDs nanoemulsion for targeted delivery of 5-fluorouracil
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jamalpoor, Zahra
Ahmadi, Hamed
Abdous, Majid
Rahdar, Abbas
coredata.dc:description: Nanoemulsions, defined as colloidal particulate systems with sizes within the submicron range, function as effective carriers for drug molecules. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), a natural non-toxic polysaccharide, extensively utilized across various fields, including drug delivery systems (DDS). CMC is a cellulose derivative with carboxymethyl groups (–CH2-COO-) attached to the cellulose backbone. The nature and degree of substitution of these carboxymethyl groups determine the charge on the CMC polymer. In aqueous solutions, the carboxymethyl groups can ionize, rendering CMC negatively charged or anionic. This study investigates the significant potential of integrating CMC, starch, silica nanoparticles (SiO2), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and the chemotherapeutic agent 5-fluorouracil into a novel nanoemulsion. The SEM analysis indicated a mean particle size of 62.45 nm, while DLS revealed a size range of 218.6–315.3 nm (hydrodynamic radius of approximately 246.98 nm) with a polydispersity index of 0.20, suggesting potential suitability for drug delivery; furthermore, a zeta potential of 35.77 mV indicated superior colloidal stability. SEM images have illustrated a core/shell structure facilitating controlled drug release. FTIR analysis has demonstrated strong interactions among the nanoemulsion components of the nanoemulsion, and X-ray diffraction has confirmed their semicrystalline nature. The system achieved unprecedented drug loading and entrapment efficiencies, 46 % and 84 % respectively. The release kinetics of drug-loaded CMC/starch/SiO2/GQDs were systematically examined at pH values of 5.4 and 7.4. The outcomes revealed an exceptional controlled pH-sensitive release profile, with accelerated release in the acid environments. In vitro cytotoxicity was assessed using MTT analysis on L929 and A549 breast cancer cells, demonstrating the nanoemulsion’s remarkable anticancer efficacy. In conclusion, the developed nanoemulsion showcases an extraordinary biocompatibility, drug-loading capability, pH-responsiveness and targeted drug release, rendering it an ideal candidate for more effective and precise cancer treatments modalities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carboxymethyl cellulose
Starch
Graphene quantum dots
Silica nanoparticles
5-fluorouracil
Nanoemulsion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical Structures of a) CMC, b) 5-FU, c) GQDS.
FTIR spectra of CMC, SiO2, Starch and GQDs.
FTIR analysis of nanocomposite before adding the nanoparticle, SiO2. after adding SiO2, and after drug loading, 5-FU.
XRD patterns of the indicated samples.
FESEM images of CMC/Starch/GQDs/SiO2@5-FU at two different magnifications.
Percentage of drug released as a function of time at pH 5.4 and 7.4.
Modelling of experimental release data to different kinetic models at pH 7.4 and pH 5.4.
Flow cytometry images of a) control, b) CMC/Starch/GQDs/SiO2, c) CMC/Starch/GQDs/SiO2/5-FU, d) free 5-FU.
MTT analysis using two cell lines.
Comparison of 5-FU loading and entrapment efficiency of different nanocarriers reported in previous works.

head.ce:title: CMC/Starch/SiO2/ GQDs nanoemulsion for targeted delivery of 5-fluorouracil

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modeling and Simulation-based Reliability and Optimization, University of Zabol, Zabol, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modeling and Simulation-based Reliability and Optimization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modeling and Simulation-based Reliability and Optimization, University of Zabol, Zabol, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanoemulsions, defined as colloidal particulate systems with sizes within the submicron range, function as effective carriers for drug molecules. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), a natural non-toxic polysaccharide, extensively utilized across various fields, including drug delivery systems (DDS). CMC is a cellulose derivative with carboxymethyl groups (–CH2-COO-) attached to the cellulose backbone. The nature and degree of substitution of these carboxymethyl groups determine the charge on the CMC polymer. In aqueous solutions, the carboxymethyl groups can ionize, rendering CMC negatively charged or anionic. This study investigates the significant potential of integrating CMC, starch, silica nanoparticles (SiO2), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and the chemotherapeutic agent 5-fluorouracil into a novel nanoemulsion. The SEM analysis indicated a mean particle size of 62.45 nm, while DLS revealed a size range of 218.6–315.3 nm (hydrodynamic radius of approximately 246.98 nm) with a polydispersity index of 0.20, suggesting potential suitability for drug delivery; furthermore, a zeta potential of 35.77 mV indicated superior colloidal stability. SEM images have illustrated a core/shell structure facilitating controlled drug release. FTIR analysis has demonstrated strong interactions among the nanoemulsion components of the nanoemulsion, and X-ray diffraction has confirmed their semicrystalline nature. The system achieved unprecedented drug loading and entrapment efficiencies, 46 % and 84 % respectively. The release kinetics of drug-loaded CMC/starch/SiO2/GQDs were systematically examined at pH values of 5.4 and 7.4. The outcomes revealed an exceptional controlled pH-sensitive release profile, with accelerated release in the acid environments. In vitro cytotoxicity was assessed using MTT analysis on L929 and A549 breast cancer cells, demonstrating the nanoemulsion’s remarkable anticancer efficacy. In conclusion, the developed nanoemulsion showcases an extraordinary biocompatibility, drug-loading capability, pH-responsiveness and targeted drug release, rendering it an ideal candidate for more effective and precise cancer treatments modalities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer, a formidable adversary in healthcare, demands continuous exploration of innovative treatment strategies. Traditional methods, including surgery, radiation therapy, and chemotherapy, are effective but often cause collateral damage on healthy tissues and trigger systemic side effects. In response to these challenges, drug delivery systems have emerged as a promising approach to enhance the precision and efficacy of cancer treatment. These systems utilize specialized carriers or materials, designed to target and transport therapeutic agents directly to cancer cells, therebyminimizing harm to surrounding healthy tissue through mechanisms such as receptor-mediated targeting [1,2]. Fluorouracil (5-FU), a commonly used chemotherapeutic agent, targets various cancer types, including those of the colorectum, breast, stomach, and pancreas, as part of chemotherapy protocols. This drug, an antimetabolite, disrupts the production of DNA and RNA, which is essential for the proliferation and survival of fast-growing cancer cells [3,4]. However, 5-FU’s action is not limited to cancerous cells; it also affects normal cells that proliferate quickly, leading to adverse effects such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, mouth ulcers, fatigue, and a heightened risk of infection. Therefore, monitoring and managing these side effects are vital to ensure the treatment’s efficacy and to preserve the patient’s quality of life and health during 5-FU therapy [5,6]. To minimize the adverse effects of 5-FU and maintain its cancer-fighting efficacy, scientists are using biochemical modulators and adjusting the dosing schedule to protect healthy cells. They are advancing in smart drug delivery technologies, aiming for precise 5-FU targeting at cancer sites, reducing harm to non-cancerous tissues [7,8]. Key innovations include nanoparticle-based vehicles like liposomes, polymeric micelles, and nanohydrogels that deliver the drug directly to cancer cells, using passive and active targeting [9]. Furthermore, the introduction of stimuli-responsive systems enables the controlled release of 5-FU, responding to tumor-specific conditions such as acidity or enzyme presence [10]. These strategies are intended to enhance 5-FU’s therapeutic index, increasing its anticancer potency while mitigating side effects, thus improving the chemotherapy process for patients [11,12].

Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) is a semi-synthetic polysaccharide derived from cellulose, extensively used in industries like pharmaceuticals. It is particularly noted for its role in developing drug delivery systems, especially for anticancer drugs like 5-FU, owing to its biocompatibility and biodegradability [13,14]. CMC’s carboxyl groups enable pH-responsive behavior, allowing for the design of drug delivery systems that release drugs in specific physiological conditions, such as in acidic tumor environments. For 5-FU, CMC enhances bioavailability and therapeutic efficacy due to its hydrophilicity and mucoadhesive properties. It can be modified for targeted delivery and controlled release, improving tumor specificity [15,16]. Research on CMC has yielded various formulations like nanoparticles, hydrogels, and polymeric micelles for 5-FU delivery, optimizing drug protection, reducing toxicity, and customizing the system for cancer treatment needs [16–18]. Utilizing starch alongside CMC enhances 5-FU delivery, with starch’s hydrogel and enzymatic breakdown aiding controlled release and increasing solubility for the water-insoluble drug. This combination boosts therapeutic efficacy by merging starch’s biocompatibility with CMC’s strength, providing adhesive and swellable properties for better mucosal attachment. The hybrid system of starch and CMC enables refined drug release timing and targeted delivery through specific interactions, offering a biocompatible, biodegradable, and adjustable platform that encapsulates and precisely delivers 5-FU, minimizing side effects and improving cancer treatment success [19–21].

Silica nanoparticles (SiO2) are pivotal in optimizing the delivery of 5-FU, an extensively utilized chemotherapeutic drug. Their considerable surface area-to-volume ratio facilitates the effective encapsulation and controlled dissemination of the drug, which may enhance its bioavailability and therapeutic impact. Additionally, the excellent biocompatibility and chemical robustness of SiO2 nanoparticles reduce unwanted reactions and preserve the drug's integrity throughout the delivery process [22,23]. Integrating SiO2 nanoparticles with carboxymethyl cellulose and starch creates a composite system that capitalizes on the distinct advantages of each constituent, resulting in a collaborative enhancement of properties. This amalgamation with biopolymers promotes a consistent drug release, augments stability, and is likely to better patient adherence by reducing the systemic toxicity often linked with Fluorouracil treatment [24–26].Incorporating Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into a delivery system for 5-FU, combined with carboxymethyl cellulose, starch, and silica nanoparticles, offers significant advantages. GQDs are renowned for their exceptional photoluminescence capabilities, making them suitable for bioimaging and monitoring the drug delivery process [27–29]. Their large surface area and customizable surface properties facilitate efficient drug encapsulation and modulated release rates. This integrated platform merges the distinct attributes of GQDs, CMC, starch, and SiO2 nanoparticles, each contributing uniquely to the system. CMC and starch ensure prolonged and stable drug release, enhancing biocompatibility, while silica nanoparticles improve the drug's stability and bioavailability. Consequently, this multifaceted approach enhances 5-FU delivery, potentially augmenting therapeutic outcomes and minimizing adverse effects [30–32].

In this study, the synergistic effects of CMC, starch, SiO2, GQDs, and 5-FU (Fig. 1 ) in the context of cancer treatment will be investigated. Although some previous works have studied the advantages of combining two of these constituents [33], the integration of all of them into a single nanoemulsion has not been accomplished before. This represents a highly innovative platform for targeted drug delivery that could provide maximized drug-loading efficiency and precise control over drug release kinetics. The combination of these materials not only promises more effective cancer therapy but also potentially reduces the burden of side effects on patients, ultimately improving their quality of life.

Moreover, targeted drug delivery mechanism enables the precise steering of drugs to specific disease sites or cancer cells, thereby minimizing collateral damage. This investigation highlights the protective effect against degradation that drug delivery systems offer to labile drugs, while also enhancing the bioavailability of compounds that are poorly soluble in water. By mitigating systemic exposure, these systems effectively reduce drug-related toxicity. This prompts a closer examination in our study to tailor these systems to the individual needs of patients. The integration of extended-release formulations, along with local treatment options, optimizes drug effectiveness by sustaining therapeutic levels and precisely delivering drugs to targeted regions. Additionally, the modulation of pharmacokinetics, as elucidated through characterization methods such as FTIR analysis and X-ray diffraction, offers flexibility in drug profiles. This presents promising avenues for personalized medicine. Overall, these attributes underscore the pivotal role of drug delivery systems in optimizing healthcare treatments and fostering patient-centric therapeutic approaches.

Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), with a degree of substitution (DS) of 0.6–0.8 and a viscosity 15,000 mPas/s (1 % in H2O at 25 °C), starch (C6H10O5)n, graphene quantum dots (GQDs, size < 5 nm, height: 1–2.0 nm), mesoporous silica nanoparticles (SiO2, 100 nm size, 30 Å pore size) and SPAN 80 surfactant were provided by Merck Company (Germany). 5-fluorouracil (5-FU, C4H3N2FO2, Mw = 130.078 g/mol) was obtained from EBEWE Pharma (Austria). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH = 5.4 and 7.4) was provided by Gibco (USA). Hazelnut oil was obtained from S.D. Fine-Chem (India). Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) was supplied by WelGENE (Daegu, Republic of Korea). The human non-small lung cancer cells (A549) and normal fibroblast mouse (L929) cells (as control) were obtained from Pasteur Institute (Tehran, Iran).All the solutions were prepared using double-distilled water.

The fabrication process involves several key steps and the careful integration of different components to form the desired nanoemulsion. Firstly, carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), a water-soluble anionic polymer, was chosen as the primary stabilizing agent due to its ability to enhance the stability and dispersion of the nanoemulsion. CMC (0.8 g) was dispersed in a 2 % w/w acetic acid (CH3COOH) solution (40 mL) and stirred at room temperature until completely dissolved, resulting in a homogeneous CMC solution (2 % w/w). Starch (0.4 g) was then added to the CMC solution as a reinforcing agent to improve the mechanical strength and structural integrity of the nanoemulsion. Starch was chosen not only for its thickening properties but also for its ability to form a rigid, interconnected network within the nanoemulsion matrix. This network can provide additional support and stability to the system. The mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 10 min to ensure thorough dispersion and homogeneity. Subsequently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanoparticles (40 mg), prepared in a separate step, were introduced into the solution. GQDs were chosen for their unique optical properties and potential applications in bioimaging and drug delivery. Silica nanoparticles (SiO2, 40 mg) were then blended into the mixture. SiO2 nanoparticles were incorporated to enhance the mechanical strength and stability of the nanoemulsion. The final solution was thoroughly stirred to achieve a uniform and homogeneous blend, resulting in a stable nanoemulsion containing CMC as the primary stabilizer, starch as the thickening agent, GQDs for their optical properties, and SiO2 nanoparticles for mechanical reinforcement.

The selected drug, 5-fluorouracil (5-FU, Fig. 1.b), was incorporated into the previously prepared mixture containing CMC, starch, GQDs, and SiO2. The drug was loaded by stirring the mixture for 30 min to achieve a final concentration of 5 µg/mL. This step resulted in the formation of a CMC/starch/GQDs/SiO2 hydrogel loaded with 5-FU. To encapsulate the drug-loaded hydrogel within nanoparticles, a two-step emulsification process was employed. First, a 5 mL syringe was used to gradually coat the hydrogel with the surfactant SPAN 80 by dripping it into 15 mL of hazelnut oil, which served as the hydrophobic phase. Continuous stirring was maintained during this step to facilitate the formation of spherical drug-loaded nanoparticles. After 10–15 min of stirring, 60 mL of deionized (DI) water was poured into the mixture, acting as the hydrophilic phase. This step allowed the formation of a water-in-oil (W/O) nanoemulsion, with the drug-loaded hydrogel suspended in the oil phase. To stabilize the nanoemulsion, the mixture was allowed to rest for 10 min without stirring, enabling phase separation. Subsequently, the oily phase containing the drug-loaded nanoparticles was separated from the hydrophilic phase using a sampler and centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the separated nanoparticles were subjected to freeze-drying to obtain a dry powder form of the drug-loaded nanoemulsion for further characterization and analysis.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was employed to characterize the functional groups and identify interactions among polymers and between polymers and the drug. The Philips PW1730 X-ray powder diffractometer operated at 25 °C, was utilized for X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements to analyze the phases of the produced nanocomposites. The scanning range for two angular regions was set from 10° to 80° [2], with a step size of 0.05°. The ZetaSizer Zen 3600 was used to determine the particle size distribution (PSD), and the zeta potential test was conducted to characterize the surface charge of nanoparticles. The analyses were performed at a scattering angle of 90° following probe sonication of the solution for 15 min. Furthermore, the surface morphology of the nanocomposite, CMC/Starch/SiO2/GQDs@5-FU, was analyzed using FESEM instrument at an appropriate magnification and a voltage of 25 kV [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of drug loading and encapsulation efficacy
ce:section.ce:para:
UV spectrophotometry (UV–Vis) was used to determine the entrapment efficacy (EE) and drug loading efficacy (DLE) of 5-FU. The absorbance was measured using a calibration curve at 266 nm, the wavelength of maximum absorption of the drug, which resulted in a regression coefficient of 99.05 %. EE and DLE were calculated using equations (1) and (2), respectively [35,36]:
(1)
%
E E
=
T o t a l a m o u n t o f 5 - F u - F r e e a m o u n t o f 5 - F u
T h e t o t a l a m o u n t o f 5 - F u
× 100
(2)
%
D L E
=
T o t a l a m o u n t o f 5 - F u - F r e e a m o u n t o f 5 - F u
ThetotalamountofNanocomposite
× 100

Using the dialysis method in phosphate-buffered media and two distinct pH levels, 7.4 and 5.4, the release of the drug from CS/SiO2/GQDs nanoemulsion was investigated. A mixture comprising 20 % v/v of ethanol and 15 ml of each buffer was prepared separately and used with 1 ml of 5-FU-loaded nanoemulsion at a temperature of 37 °C. At predetermined time intervals of 0, 12, 24, 48, 72, and 96 h, 300 ml of the mixture was sampled and analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 370 nm to determine the quantity of 5-FU released into the buffer. After each sampling, an equivalent volume of fresh PBS was added to maintain the volume and concentration, ensuring the maintenance of sink conditions with ethyl alcohol and the continuous renewal of the PBS [37]. The amount of 5-FU released was calculated using Equation (3):
(3)
5 - F U r e l
( % )
=
( 5 - F U )
rel
( 5 - F U )
load
× 100
The amount of 5-FU encapsulated within the nanoemulsion is denoted as
( 5 - F U )
load
, while the quantity of 5-FU released from the nanoemulsion is indicated as
( 5 - F U )
rel
.

The investigation into the release mechanism of 5-FU from the nanocomposite at pH 7.4 and pH 5.4 involved defining the release information according to various models in the experiment, including zero-order, first-order, Higuchi models, Weibull, Hixson and Korsmeyer-Peppas models [38,39]. The coefficient of correlation (R2), which is used to identify the best-fit model, indicates the goodness of fit.

The zero-order model is used to describe drug release kinetics where the rate of release is constant over time, regardless of the drug concentration. This model is particularly relevant for controlled-release formulations where a consistent drug release rate is desired to maintain therapeutic drug levels in the bloodstream [39]:
Q t
=
Q 0
+
k 0
t
At time “t,” the cumulative amount of released drug is denoted by Q t. Q 0 stands for the initial amount of the drug, and the zero-order release is characterized by the zero-order release constant. The zero-order rate constant, k, defines the steady rate at which the drug is dispensed from the formulation, a rate that remains unaffected by the remaining drug concentration in the delivery system.

Modeling the release of drugs from the pores of nanocomposites, particularly for water-soluble medications, involves a commonly used semi-empirical kinetic model known as the first-order model. This model is typically used when the drug release process is concentration-dependent and not solely governed by diffusion:
Ln
Q t
= L n
Q 0
+
k 1
t
The total release of the drug at the time t is represented by
Q t
, the starting quantity of the drug is denoted as Q0, and constant of the first-order release is k1 [40]. The first-order rate constant, denoted by k, reflects the speed at which the drug exits the formulation, with a larger k value indicating a quicker release rate.

To investigate the release of water-soluble and poorly water-soluble molecules from matrices the Higuchi-model is used:
M t
M ∞
=
k h
t
0.5
The amount of drug released during time t is represented as
M t
M ∞
and the Higuchi release constant is denoted by
k h
. The quantity of drug released, denoted by
M t
M ∞
, is directly related to the square root of time, indicating a decrease in the release rate over time due to the diminishing concentration gradient. In the Higuchi model for drug release kinetics,
k h
denotes the Higuchi dissolution constant. An increased
k h
value signifies a more rapid diffusion of the drug through the matrix, leading to a quicker release from the formulation [41,42].

The Weibull model is a flexible statistical method used to study drug release kinetics, explaining the release mechanisms and patterns, and is suitable for analyzing both dissolution and controlled-release systems. The equation of the Weibull model is as follows [43]:
F
( t )
= 1 - e x p ( 1 -
( 1 -
t τ
β
)
In the Weibull model,
F ( t )
represents the fraction of the drug released at time t, τ is the scale parameter signifying the time required for 63.2 % of the drug to be released, β is the shape parameter that denotes the drug release mechanism, and t is the time variable. The τ parameter is associated with the duration of the drug release process, serving as an indicator of release rate, with lower values of τ signifying quicker drug dispersal [44,45].

The Baker-Lonsdale model serves as a mathematical tool tailored for analyzing the kinetics of drug release from spherical controlled-release entities like microcapsules and microspheres. It builds on the Higuchi equation, integrating the spherical shape of the drug particles to provide a more accurate depiction of their release dynamics in such systems. The formula representing the Baker-Lonsdale model is [45]:
M t
M ∞
=
3
r
2
( k t +
1 - k t
exp
- k t
)
In the Baker-Lonsdale model,
M t
M ∞
represents the fraction of the drug released at time t, with k being the constant that denotes the rate of release, t as the time duration, and r as the radius of the spherical particles. The term
M t
M ∞
reflects the total amount of drug released as a percentage of the overall drug quantity. The constant k determines the speed of the release, where a higher k signifies a quicker drug release. The time factor t accounts for the duration over which the release is monitored. The radius r, pertaining to the size of the particles, affects how quickly the drug is released, with smaller particles leading to faster release owing to their greater surface area to volume ratio [39,46].

The Hixson-Crowell model, also known as the cube root law, is utilized to analyze the kinetics of drug release, focusing on scenarios where the rate of dissolution correlates with the surface area and the volume alteration of the particles. It is particularly relevant when the reduction in particle size, and consequently surface area, dictates the rate at which the drug is released. The mathematical expression for the Hixson-Crowell model is provided as follows [47]:
W
0
1 / 3
-
W
t
1 3
) = k t
Within the Hixson-Crowell model framework, W 0 indicates the initial drug amount in its pharmaceutical form, while W t denotes the drug quantity remaining at the specific time t. The term k is identified as the Hixson-Crowell rate constant, and t reflects the passage of time. The model uses
W
0
1 / 3
-
W
t
1 / 3
to represent the change in the cube roots of the initial and remaining amounts of the drug, indicating the volume change as the drug particles dissolve. The rate constant k is crucial for gauging the speed at which the drug is released, incorporating both dissolution and diffusion effects, with a higher k value indicating a more rapid release. The time variable t is used to track the timeframe of the release process [48,49].

The Korsmeyer-Peppas design model is a comprehensive yet straightforward empirical equation that can be utilized to model the mechanisms of drug release when the release rate is influenced by multiple processes [50,51]:
Mt/M∞ = k.tn.

In the context of drug release, the fraction of drug released at time t is denoted as M t/M ∞, where k represents the Korsmeyer-Peppas release constant, and n is the release exponent. if n equals 0.5, the predominant mechanism of release is Fickian diffusion, wherein the rate of release is controlled by the diffusion of the solvent. When the value of “n” is between 0.5 and 1 (0.5 < n < 1), the release mechanism is considered anomalous or non-Fickian, indicating that both diffusion and polymer relaxation processes govern the release rate. This is due to the transition state dynamics and swelling of polymers in response to solvent penetration [40].

The cytotoxicity assessment of the nanocomposite involved conducting the MTT assay on human lung cancer cells (A549) and normal mouse fibroblast (L929) cells. The cells were cultured in a high-glucose DMEM environment supplemented with 1 % (v/v) penicillin–streptomycin and 10 % (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS). A 96-well tissue culture plate was used, seeding each well with 100 µL of an MCF-7 suspension containing 5 × 103 cells. Incubation occurred at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 in a humidified atmosphere for 24 h. Following this, the cells were exposed to CMC/Starch/SiO2/GQDs, CMC/Starch/SiO2/GQDs@5-FU, and free 5-FU, with subsequent comparison of results against the untreated control group [52].

After 48-hour, the wells were washed, and subsequently 100 µL of the media containing 5000 ppm MTT reagent was introduced into each well for a 4-hour incubation. Following this, 200 µL of DMSO was applied to dissolve the resulting crystals. After carefully removing the medium, the absorbance at 570 nm was quantified using a microplate reader. The equation below was utilized to calculate the relative cell viability for each treatment:
(4)
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
( % )
=
( A b s o r b a n c e )
t r e a t e d
g r o u p
( A b s o r b a n c e )
c o n t r o l
g r o u p

To investigate the observed cytotoxicity’s nature, specifically whether it originates from apoptosis or necrosis, flow cytometry was utilized. Human lung cancer cells (A549) were cultured in 6-well plates at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well. Cells underwent treatment for 24 h with various agents: free 5-FU, CMC/Starch/GQDs/SiO2, CMC/Starch/GQDs/SiO2@5-FU, unloaded CMC/Starch/GQDs/SiO2, and encapsulated 5-FU at a concentration of 7 µg/ml. An untreated group acted as the control. Following treatment, cells were washed with PBS, resuspended in 100 µL binding buffer, incubated for 48 h, and then harvested. Staining involved Propidium Iodide (PI) and Annexin V-fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC), with a subsequent incubation in darkness at room temperature for 15 min. Analysis of the prepared samples was performed using a flow cytometer (BD Biosciences, USA) [53].

As illustrated in Fig. 2 , the FTIR analysis of CMC demonstrates several significant peaks: the presence of O–H stretching of free hydroxyl groups within the range of 3200–3600 cm-1, C–H stretching of alkyl groups within the range of 2850–2920 cm-1, C = O stretching of carbonyl groups within the range of 1600–1750 cm-1, and C-O stretching of ether and ester groups within the range of 1000–1300 cm-1 [54]. SiO2 displays Si-O-Si stretching typically observed around 1000–1100 cm-1 and, if present, exhibits O–H stretching of surface hydroxyl groups within the range of 3200–3600 cm-1. GQDs might exhibit C = O stretching (if present on the surface) within the range of 1600–1750 cm-1 and C-O stretching (if present on the surface) in the range of 1000–1300 cm-1. On the other hand, Flour Starch displays O–H stretching of free hydroxyl groups within the range of 3200–3600 cm-1, C–H stretching of alkyl groups within the range of 2850–2920 cm-1, C = O stretching of carbonyl groups within the range of 1600–1750 cm-1, and C-O stretching of ether and ester groups within the range of 1000–1300 cm-1 [55,56]. When these compounds are mixed, the FTIR analysis would reveal the combined characteristic peaks and bands of all the individual components.

The FTIR analysis of the blend of CMC and Starch (CMC/Starch) is expected to reveal characteristic peaks and bands that represent both components (see Fig. 3 ). The O–H stretching of free hydroxyl groups, originating from CMC and Starch, should appear within the range of 3200–3600 cm-1. Additionally, the C–H stretching of alkyl groups from both components could be observed in the range of 2850–2920 cm-1. Similarly, the C = O stretching of carbonyl groups from both CMC and Starch is anticipated to be evident within the range of 1600–1750 cm-1. Furthermore, the C-O stretching of ether and ester groups from both components may be discernible in the range of 1000–1300 cm-1.

Furthermore, in the mixed compound, interactions between the hydroxyl groups of CMC and Starch in the Fig. 3, are expected to occur, potentially leading to hydrogen bonding between the two components. The combination of these interactions and characteristic peaks would provide insights into the structural changes and potential new functionalities resulting from the CMC/Starch mixture [57].

The interactions between the compounds in the mixture can be inferred based on their functional groups and potential bonding sites. When CMC and SiO2 are combined, the hydroxyl groups from CMC may interact with the surface hydroxyl groups of SiO2 through hydrogen bonding. Additionally, the ether and ester groups of CMC can interact with the Si-O-Si groups of SiO2 through dipole–dipole interactions [58]. In the case of the mixture involving GQDs and Flour Starch, the carbonyl groups from both GQDs and Starch could interact through dipole–dipole interactions or hydrogen bonding [59]. Furthermore, the C-O stretching vibrations of both compounds might lead to interactions through hydrogen bonding. Upon the amalgamation of all four compounds, multiple simultaneous interactions can occur. For instance, hydrogen bonding might arise between the hydroxyl groups of CMC and SiO2. Moreover, interactions between the carbonyl groups of GQDs and Starch can occur. These interactions could collectively influence the overall behavior and properties of the compound mixture. Dipole-dipole interactions between the different functional groups present in the mixture can contribute to the overall stability and characteristics of the compound combination [60].

Analysis of the nanocomposite CMC/Starch/GQDs before and after adding the SiO2 nanoparticle, as well as drug loading with 5-FU, is illustrated in Fig. 4 and interpreted below. The main peaks of the CMC/Starch/GQDs nanocomposite are indicated before and after adding SiO2; the transmittance percentages peak at 1099.32 cm-1 and 480–170 cm-1, representing the Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching and bending vibrations, respectively. Specific vibrations such as Si-O-(H-H2O) bending (observed near 966.37 cm-1) and in-plane bending vibrations of germinal groups (at 804 cm-1) are also noted. Peaks at 1099.32 cm-1 denote Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching, while the presence of surface –OH groups is indicated near 3454.31 cm-1, coupled with the bending of these –OH groups [61]. After loading 5-FU, specific peaks observed include a sharp band around 1700–1800 cm-1 attributed to C = O stretching from the carbonyl group. Additionally, there are peaks in the range of 1250–1650 cm-1, which represent the C-N and C–C stretching within the pyrimidine ring. Another characteristic peak appears around 3100–3500 cm-1, indicating N–H stretching in the amino group [62]. The pronounced bimodal peak in the spectral range of 1732 to 1622 cm−1 signifies the presence of carbonyl groups in the GQDs and CMC matrix. Upon integrating 5-FU, the dual-peak structure coalesces into a singular peak, reflecting alterations in the chemical milieu of the carbonyl functionalities. This modification likely results from the interaction between 5-FU and the carbonyl groups within the composite, possibly through hydrogen bonding or a chemical reaction, manifesting as a shift or transformation in the absorption properties of the C = O bond [63]. Furthermore, an enhancement in the peak intensity at 1622 cm−1 may denote the incorporation of 5-FU, which possesses two carbonyl groups, thus altering the spectroscopic fingerprint of the material.

XRD analysis is vital for elucidating the structural features and phase behavior of a nanoemulsion system containing carboxymethyl cellulose, starch, SiO2, GQDs, and 5-FU, crucial for determining its physicochemical properties. The XRD analysis of nanocomposites, including CMC, CMC/Starch, CMC/Starch/GQDs, and CMC/Starch/SiO2/GQDs@5-FU, is presented in Fig. 5 . The sharp and distinct peaks observed at 2θ values of 22°, 35.84°, 45.59°, 54.14°, 62.39°, and 63.49° in the XRD analysis indicate the hexagonal phase of hematite. This observation supports the semi-crystalline nature of CMC, which is attributed to the formation of hydrogen bond [64].

The addition of amorphous Starch to CMC results in a decrease in the peak intensity ratio of CMC/Starch, as seen in Fig. 6 . This decrease indicates the high solubility and complex formation between the two polymers. Fig. 6 also demonstrates that the desired peaks are present in the XRD patterns without a shift in position, indicating the successful incorporation of GQDs nanoparticles into the chitosan and SiO2 polymer matrix. Despite no significant differences in XRD patterns at various composite stages, this analysis provides key insights into molecular arrangement and crystallinity, affecting the system’s stability, drug encapsulation, and release properties. XRD ensures the structural integrity and uniformity of the nanoemulsion, maintaining the crystalline structure upon Fluorouracil integration, essential for a stable and effective drug delivery system. Subtle variations in the diffraction patterns highlight minor structural changes or interactions between components, enhancing understanding of the nanoemulsion’s functionality. Thus, XRD stands as an indispensable tool in characterizing and optimizing the nanoemulsion for pharmaceutical applications [65–67].The variations in peak intensities, as shown in the plots, provide additional evidence of the good miscibility between the medication and the nanocomposite, corroborating the FTIR results [33,68].

In our investigation of the CMC/Starch/SiO2/GQDs@5-FU nanoemulsion, the use of dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) enabled us to analyze its size distribution (Fig. 6). The study employed the multi-exponential autocorrelation function method for DLS analysis to elucidate the size distribution of polydisperse particles. Employing a multi-exponential autocorrelation function for DLS analysis enhances the accuracy in distinguishing various particle sizes within nanocomposites, particularly beneficial for polydisperse samples. The numerical weighting method effectively accentuates the significance of each particle size fraction, yielding a more accurate representation of the size distribution. This methodology substantially improves the precision and dependability of DLS measurements, essential for the comprehensive characterization of nanocomposite materials. The DLS examination revealed that the nanoemulsions had a size range of 218.6–315.3 nm and a polydispersity index of 0.20, indicating their suitability for drug delivery applications. The measurements revealed a hydrodynamic radius of approximately 246.98 nm when the nanocomposite was suspended in a solution. Notably, we observed a maximum frequency of 57.22 % and recorded a maximum particle surface potential of 35.77 mV. These parameters are indicative of the nanocomposite’s stability within an aqueous solution, a pivotal characteristic underscored by the detailed insights illustrated in the accompanying Fig. 7
[64,69,70].

The hydrodynamic radius of the suspension is approximately 246.98 nm, which falls within the desirable size range of 200–300 nm for drug delivery systems, enhancing cellular uptake and circulation in the bloodstream. This size is particularly appropriate for the 5-FU drug delivery, facilitating its application in medical treatments. Additionally, a maximum frequency of 57.22 % suggests a predominant size distribution within this range, implying a narrow and uniform size distribution of the nanocomposite particles. The zeta potential of 35.77 mV further confirms the colloidal stability of the suspension, attributed to strong electrostatic repulsive forces that minimize particle aggregation or flocculation [71–73]. These characteristics underline the starch/SiO2/GQDs@5-FU CMC nanoemulsion’s potential as an effective medium for drug delivery, characterized by its optimal size, uniform distribution, and stable colloidal nature.

The generation of a positive zeta charge can be attributed to several factors. Notably, SPAN 80, despite being a nonionic surfactant, plays a pivotal role in the nanoemulsion preparation process. At a neutral pH, SPAN 80′s hydroxyl (OH) groups undergo protonation, resulting in the formation of hydroxonium ions (OH2 +), thereby inducing a slight positive charge. The specific adsorption orientation of SPAN 80 at the oil–water interface brings its slightly protonated hydrophilic heads into contact with the aqueous phase, which can confer a positive charge to the nanoemulsion droplets. Additionally, polysaccharides like starch and CMC, which possess hydroxyl groups, have the potential to be protonated, thus contributing to the emergence of positively charged species [74–76].

FESEM analysis was conducted to examine the morphology of the CMC/Starch/SiO2/GQDs@5-FU nanoemulsion, as shown in Fig. 8 . SEM images reveal that the drug-loaded nanoemulsions consist of nearly spherical droplets, with liquid phases encapsulated by surfactants. These images exhibit a clear and relatively uniform distribution of nano-sized droplets, indicating a well-formulated nanoemulsion. Despite occasional clustering observed in certain areas, the nanoemulsions retain their individual, rounded shapes, thanks to the excellent stability of the formulation, which prevents droplet merging. Notably, due to the pH sensitivity of these nanoemulsions, they maintain their integrity until reaching the cancer cell site, where they release the drug payload effectively [69].

The analysis indicates that, on the nanometer scale, nanoemulsion sizes range from 34.86 nm to 99.72 nm, with an average of 62.45 nm and a standard deviation of 17.41 nm. These metrics affirm the particles’ appropriateness for the intended nanoparticle applications. Fig. 9 presents a bar graph depicting the size distribution at this scale. The uniform surface appearance suggests an effective blend of polymers at a precise 1:1 wt ratio, likely a result of chemical bonding during the cross-linking process. Such homogeneity prevents the emergence of island-like structures, thereby enhancing the overall uniformity and compatibility of the nanoparticles [70,77].

Entrapment efficiency (EE%) and drug loading efficiency (DLE%) were precisely evaluated to determine the influence of SiO2 nanoparticles on our composite’s performance. The incorporation of SiO2 led to an EE of 83.75 %, marking a substantial increase from the 72.06 % noted in nanocarriers devoid of silica. This enhancement is likely due to SiO2′s extensive surface area and porosity, offering additional interaction and encapsulation sites for the drug, thus improving EE%. In contrast, nanocarriers lacking SiO2 showed reduced efficiency, missing out on these critical drug-binding sites essential for optimal encapsulation [70,78]. The addition of SiO2 also improved DLE, raising it from 34.20 % to 46.05 %. This increment is attributed to the combined influences of SiO2′s structural attributes, the viscosity and stabilizing effects of CMC, and the distinctive properties of GQDs and starch. In this composite, CMC enhances medium viscosity, aiding in drug maintenance; GQDs ensure nanoparticle stability and even distribution; and starch serves as an effective binder and volumizer, augmenting the structure for better drug containment. The synergistic interaction among these components, particularly with SiO2′s addition, significantly bolsters drug entrapment and loading, thereby amplifying the nanocomposite’s efficiency for medical applications. This holistic strategy highlights the vital enhancements in drug containment and efficacy brought about by SiO2 and the collaborative effects of the composite’s ingredients [64,70,78].

Table 1 compares the drug loading and entrapment efficiency of different nanocarriers reported in the literature, including CMC, SiO2 or GQDs, with those obtained in this study. It can be observed that the EE achieved in the work with the presence of SiO2 is notably higher than that reported for the other nanocarriers. This finding corroborates the effectiveness of the nanocomposite developed in this study for encapsulating the drug. The very large surface area and porous nature of silica, combined with the synergistic effect of the other nanoemulsion constituents, should account for the observed improvement.

The dialysis technique was utilized to monitor the 5-FU release from the nanoemulsion at 37 °C for 96 h at pH levels of 7.4 and 5.4 (see Fig. 10 ). At pH 7.4, the CMC/Starch/GQDs/SiO2 exhibited a gradual and almost continuous release profile, releasing 4 % of their 5-FU after 24 h. However, in the acidic medium (pH 5.4), the release rate was faster, yet still considered desirable when compared to that at pH 7.4. After 96 h, these nanocomposites released 95.5 % of their 5-FU, demonstrating pH-sensitive behavior. Notably, at pH = 5.4, 68 % of 5-FU was released after 96 h, with the release rate maintaining nearly constant levels after 72 h. In contrast, at pH 7.4, only 84 % of the 5-FU was released during the same period. These results highlight a sustained release pattern over 96 h, suggesting the potential for extended drug release. Such a sustained release could lead to decreased frequency of drug administration, ensuring adequate drug concentrations are maintained for an extended period [70].

The process of fitting the experimental data into the distinctive kinetic models tailored for the varying pH conditions yielded compelling results. Specifically, the utilization of the Zero-Order model to describe the release dynamics at pH 7.4 showcased a robust correlation, boasting a high coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.966, as shown in Fig. 11 . Further, the application of the Higuchi model, specifically tailored for the release kinetics under the acidic pH 5.4, displayed an even more striking alignment between the predicted and observed values, showcasing a notably high R2 of 0.997. These high coefficients of determination, especially the 0.997 obtained at pH 5.4, underscore a remarkably close match between the anticipated release patterns derived from the models and the actual experimental outcomes, highlighting the precision and reliability of these models in characterizing the release behavior at their respective pH environments [85]. The Zero-Order kinetic model at pH 7.4, with an R2 of 0.966, indicates a significant correlation, demonstrating a constant release rate of the substance that does not depend on its concentration. This consistent release could be attributed to the physicochemical properties of the substance or the environment of its encapsulation, promoting steady release at physiological pH. These results are crucial for pharmaceutical applications, where it’s important to maintain consistent drug levels for therapeutic efficacy. The strong correlation with the Zero-Order model helps elucidate the release process and supports its use for reliable drug delivery in biological environments [86–88]. The outstanding match (R2 = 0.997) between the expected and actual data in the Higuchi model at pH 5.4 highlights a drug release primarily governed by diffusion, influenced by the acidic setting that possibly alters matrix features like swelling, erosion, or solubility. Such alterations promote a uniform diffusion process, in line with the premises of the Higuchi model. The model's strong predictive capability can be attributed to the acidic pH boosting the drug’s solubility and ensuring a consistent concentration gradient, thus establishing diffusion as the main driver of drug release [89–91].

Flow cytometry was used to determine the percentages of cell apoptosis and necrosis following the treatment with CMC/Starch/GQDs/SiO2@5-FU and 5-FU drug. Data from Fig. 12 segmented cells into distinct categories: necrotic (FITC-/PI + ), late apoptotic (FITC+/PI + ), early apoptotic (FITC+/PI-), and viable (FITC-/PI-). Changes in each cell phase reflected different types of programmed cell deaths. The findings revealed that the group treated with CMC/Starch/GQDs/SiO2@5-FU and 5-FU displayed nominal alterations in cell viability compared to the control group, demonstrating the biocompatibility of the CMC/Starch/GQDs/SiO2 nanocomposite and the limited efficacy of the free 5-FU drug. Notably, the CMC/Starch/GQDs/SiO2@5-FU nanocomposite exhibited the highest rate of apoptosis induction, surpassing the individual components. This suggests enhanced cellular uptake and efficacy of the nanocomposite in inducing programmed cell deaths. Comparatively, similar studies have highlighted the effectiveness of composite-based drug delivery systems in promoting apoptosis in cancer cells [92]. Additionally, similar studies have found that 5-FU within nanocarrier induces apoptosis more effectively than its pure form in different cancer cell lines [93,94].

To investigate the cytotoxicity of the different samples, MTT assays were carried out on human lung cancer cells (A549) and normal mouse fibroblast (L929) cells (Fig. 13 ). The control group, which had not been treated with any material, was used to compare the cytotoxicity of the samples. Cell viability of A549 was around 99 % for CMC and CMC/starch, and 97 % for CMC/starch/GQDs and CMC/starch/GQDs/SiO2, indicating that these samples exhibited no substantial cytotoxicity. According to ISO standards (ISO, 10993–5, 2009), nanocomposites with a cell viability higher than 80 % can be regarded as non-cytotoxic. The observed drop in cell viability to approximately 50 % with the integration of 5-FU into the composite highlights the nanocarrier system's targeted delivery and superior effectiveness over the 60 % viability seen with standalone 5-FU. This reduction indicates that the nanocomposite promotes a more concentrated delivery of the drug to cancer cells. Additionally, the nanocomposite’s slow release mechanism likely contributes to a more regulated therapeutic impact, reducing the likelihood of swift drug expulsion and thereby increasing chemotherapy's effectiveness. The interaction between 5-FU and the cancer cells, potentially enhanced by the composite’s ability to improve drug penetration and retention, demonstrates the promise of CMC/starch/GQDs/SiO2 as a cutting-edge vehicle for water-insoluble medications like 5-FU. This method of gradual drug release stands in stark contrast to the rapid discharge common in conventional chemotherapy treatments, resulting in better penetration and retention of 5-FU within the cancer cells, as our findings suggest. Ultimately, our data support the theory that the amalgamation of CMC, starch, GQDs, and SiO2 into a singular composite material bolsters the anti-cancer properties of 5-FU, while simultaneously reducing its toxicity to non-cancerous cells. This comprehensive strategy enhances the solubility and administration of the drug, further highlighting the composite’s role as a safer, more efficient approach for cancer treatment.

This research delineates the development of a CMC/Starch/SiO2/GQDs@5-FU nanoemulsion, specifically engineered for the effective delivery of 5-fluorouracil (5-FU). Analytical characterization through Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray Diffraction (XRD) verified the successful incorporation of 5-FU into the nanocomposite structure. The Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) technique identified the nanoemulsion's particle size within the optimal range of 218.6–315.3 nm for drug delivery, combined with a polydispersity index of 0.20 and a stable zeta potential of 35.77 mV, indicating its high suitability for therapeutic use. Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) demonstrated the nanoemulsion's spherical morphology and its pH-responsive nature, ensuring effective release of the drug at specific target sites. The addition of silica nanoparticles (SiO2) significantly increased entrapment efficiency to 83.75 % and drug loading efficiency to 46.05 %, due to SiO2′s large surface area and porosity, enhancing drug interaction and encapsulation. The nanoemulsion showcased a controlled and pH-sensitive release profile, with accelerated drug release in acidic environments, aligning with the needs of targeted cancer treatment. Release kinetics were consistent with the Zero-Order and Higuchi models, confirming a reliable and predictable release. In vitro cytotoxicity tests showed that the nanoemulsion induced significant apoptosis in cancer cells while exhibiting minimal toxicity towards normal cells, underscoring its precision in targeted delivery and therapeutic efficacy. In conclusion, the CMC/Starch/SiO2/GQDs@5-FU nanoemulsion is an advanced drug delivery system that offers optimal particle size, controlled and targeted release, and improved cellular uptake, enhancing the effectiveness of 5-FU in cancer treatment and reducing its adverse side effects.

Zahra Jamalpoor: Formal analysis, Data curation. Hamed Ahmadi: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Majid Abdous:Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Abbas Rahdar: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design, Synthesis, and Characterization of Linear and Branched Semifluorinated Polymers for Therapeutic and Diagnostic Drug Delivery Applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Tangsangasaksri, Design, Synthesis, and Characterization of Linear and Branched Semifluorinated Polymers for Therapeutic and Diagnostic Drug Delivery Applications. The University of Wisconsin-Madison, 2020.
H. Zhong, L. Yang, G. Zeng, M.L. Brusseau, Y. Wang, Y. Li, Z. Liu, X. Yuan, F. Tan, RSC Adv. 5 (2015) 78142-78149.
